Title: Johnny Caught a Flea
Source: Gagné, D., & Sills, J. (2011). The Orff Source. Alberta, Themes and Variations.
Game:
Students form a standing circle.
A "Johnny" is selected to walk around the outside of the circle. Johnny chooses a student by tapping them on the shoulder and the chosen student lies down pretending to be the dead flea. Johnny rejoins the circle and a new Johnny is chosen by the teacher.
Full Rhythm: t t t z tt tt t z t t tt t t t t z
PDFs: Johnny Caught a Flea-Traditional_Notation.pdf, Johnny Caught a Flea-Stick_Notation.pdf, Johnny Caught a Flea-Stick_Notation_no_melody.pdf

Title: Mouse, Mousie
Source: Choksy, L. (1981). The Kodaly Context. Upper Saddle River, NJ: Prentice Hall
Game:
Students form a seated circle. 
A "Mousie" is selected by the teacher and stands and closes their eyes. A "Kitty" is secretly selected by the teacher (optionally with all students' eyes closed). Mousie walks around the outside of the circle while students sing. On 'you', Kitty jumps up and runs around the circle to chase Mousie. Mousie can win by sitting in Kitty's spot. Win or lose, the Kitty becomes the new Mousie and a new Kitty is secretly chosen by the teacher.
Full Rhythm: t tt tt tt t t tt t tt tt tt tt t t tt t
PDFs: Mouse Mousie-Traditional_Notation.pdf, Mouse Mousie-Stick_Notation.pdf, Mouse Mousie-Stick_Notation_no_melody.pdf
Lyrics: Mouse, mousie, little mousie, hurry, hurry do. Or the kitty in the housie will be chasing you!

Title: Paw Paw Patch
Source: Rohrbough, L. (1940). Hand Play Party Book. Delaware, OH. Cooperative Recreation Service.
Game:
Students form two lines (Line 1 & Line 2), all facing forward.
Verse 1 = Student 1 in Line 2 walks clockwise around Line 2 and Line 1 and back to original spot.
Verse 2 = Same student walks around again, this time with all of the students in Line 1 following behind.
Verse 3 = Line 1 walks counterclockwise to the bottom of the line and back up again to the same spots at the same time as Line 2 walks clockwise to the bottom of their line and back up again to the same spots. When the first student of each line meets in their original spots again, they face each other, join hands, and form an arch. The rest of the students go under the arch and go around each line again and back up to where they started. The first student in each line goes to the bottom of the lines and there is a new student in the front of each line.
source: bethsnotesplus.com
Full Rhythm: tt tt tktk tt tt tt tktk tt tt tt tktk tt tt tktk tt t

Title: Tideo
Source: Owens, W. (1936). Swing and Turn: Texas Play-Party Games. Dallas, TX. Tardy Publishing Co. p. 83.
Classroom Use: Introduce tika-tika, Practice high do, Practice form
Game:
Students form two concentric circles, facing a partner. 
On every ‘pass', the outer circle steps one partner to the left. On every ‘Tideo', pat knees, clap hands, clap partner to the rhythm. On every ‘Jingle at the window', pat knees to the rhythm. 
Increase the difficulty: ‘Pass 1 window', outer circles steps 1 partner to the left. ‘Pass 2 windows', step 2 parters. ect.

Title: Vamos a Jugar
Source: Collected by Sally Trenfield, in 1995, from her students in Texas.
Game:
Students form a seated circle, with student's left hands facing up and right hand, palm-up, in their neighbor's left hand.
Students pass the beat by crossing their body with their right hand and tapping the hand of the neighbor to their left. At the end of the song, the student just tapped chooses a number 1-10 in Spanish. Students count up to the chosen number while continuing the pass hand pattern. The final student tapped is out, and can create a new circle inside the larger circle.

Title: Here Comes the Mailman
Source: Learned from Christopher Roberts. 2023. Kodaly Levels class at SPU.
Game:
Students form a seated circle. 
A mailman is chosen by the teacher. They are given ‘mail' that can be any small trinket. Sitting students close their eyes and sing with their hands cupped in front of them. Even students who did not get a delivery peer into their hand to try to deceive their peers. Teacher: “Who has the (penny)?” [smlsm t tt t t]  Solo student: “I have the (penny)” [smlsm t tt t t]. A new mailman is chosen by the teacher.

Title: Rocky Road
Source: Bennett, L. (1957). Children's Jamaican Songs and Games. Sound Recording, FW 7250. Transcribed C. Roberts June, 2014.
Type: Call and Response, Play Party, Gate Game
Game:
Students form two lines facing each other with raised hands to form an arch.
The couple at one end drops hands and trots through the arch to the other and form an arch when theу get there. The other couples follow in succession, the lines moving towards the original starting point of the game. That means that the lines are always moving until the point. The first couple gets back to its original starting point. The first couple now drops hands and trots, each on the again outside of his line, towards the end, where they face each other and form an arch. The other couples do the same thing until the first couple is again in its original position, when the game ends. 
source: Bennett, L. (1957). Children's Jamaican Songs and Games.

Title: Alabama Gal
Source: Eisenberg, L. & Seeger, P. (1959). American Play Parties. Folkways FC 7604.
Classroom Use: Practice syn-co-pa, Introduce syn-co-pa, Practice low so
Game:
Students form two lines facing each other, about six feet apart.
Verse 1: Head couple join hands and sashay down the center of the set toward the foot for 8 beats (“Come through 'n a hurry” twice). They return to the head of the set during the next 8 beats.
Verses 2–4: Head couple reel down the line. At the end of the fourth verse, the singing begins again with “Come through 'n a hurry.” At this point the second couple begins the figures with a sashay down the set, going as far as the first couple if that couple has not yet finished reeling to the bottom. Continue the dance until all couples have taken part.
source: kodalycollection.org, Eisenberg, L. & Seeger, P. (1959). American Play Parties. Folkways FC 7604.
Full Rhythm: scp t t scp t t scp t t tt tt t z

Title: Billy Billy
Source: Locke, E. (1979). American Folk Songs for Teaching. Oakland,CA: Holy Names College
Game:
Students form two lines of partners (A and B), facing each other.
Verse 1: Partners join hands and move arms in a sawing motion.
Verse 2: Partners drop hands and step back on the beat, clapping on the offbeat.
Verse 3: On “Walkin' down the alley,” (A) of the head couple struts down the alley and proceeds to the foot of own line. Others continue clapping on the offbeat.
Verse 4: (B) of the head couple struts down the alley, mimicking the strut of (A), and proceeds to the end of own line. Others continue clapping on the offbeat.
source: kodalycollection.org, Locke, E. (1979). American Folk Songs for Teaching. Oakland,CA: Holy Names College

Title: Chase That Squirrel
Source: Piper, E. (1915). Play-Party Games of the Middle West. Champaign, IL. Journal of American Folklore.
Classroom Use: Practice low so, Practice tim-ka, Practice tika-ti
Game:
Students form two lines facing each other, optionally separating into boys and girls.
The lead couple promenades down the center during stanza 1. Then the girl runs around the rank of girls, the boy around the rank of boys. The boy attempts to overtake the girl in her dash down the lane—a feat not always easy, since the boys have extended their rank to lengthen his course.

Title: Ickle Ockle
Source: Opie, P. & Opie, I. (1969). Children's Games in Street and Playground
Game:
Possible game: Students pair with a partner in a standing circle.
M. 1 - Partners join hands, sway left and right to the beat
M. 3 - Students individually rotate around one time
M. 3 - Same as M. 1
M. 5 Students join hands and twist with arms around their heads, “wringing the dishrag”
End - Students scatter to find a new parter
With an odd number of students, the final student with no partner can walk around the circle during the song to wait for a new partner, and a new student will become the odd one out to walk around the circle next time.

Title: Frog in the Meadow
Source: Schinhan, J. (1962). Frank C. Brown Collection of North Carolina Folklore, Vol.V. Durham, NC. Duke University Press
Classroom Use: Introduce re, Introduce rest, Practice rest
Game:
Students form a seated circle. A “Frog” is chosen to stand in the center.
During the song, the Frog rotates in place pointing outward towards the students. At the end, the Frog stops spinning and their finger stops and points at two students. The two students stand and the Frog stands between them. On ‘go', the students race outside the circle in opposite directions away from the Frog. The first student around the circle to make it back to the Frog is the winner and becomes the new Frog. Note: when the runners are going around the circle, they must pass each other. Encourage them to high-five each other to avoid a froggy collision.
Full Rhythm: t tt t t t tt t z tt tt t t t tt t z

Students are seated in front of the whiteboard.

Title: Zapatitos Blancos
Source: Montoya-Stier, G. (2008). El Patio de mi Casa: 42 traditional rhymes, chants, and folk songs from Mexico. Chicago, IL. GIA. p. 20
Game:
Students form a seated circle with both legs extended in front of them. The teacher or a student leader is chosen to be inside the circle.
The leader pats a steady beat around the circle, tapping each student's shoe as everyone speaks the rhyme. On the last beat of the rhyme, the student on whom the beat lands says how old they are. The beat continues around the circle for however old the student is. (For example, if the student is seven, the beat passes seven more shoes.) 
When the beat lands on the final shoe, that person's foot is eliminated from the game. They tuck it underneath them, out of the circle, so one foot is still in the game and one foot is out. Continue until all shoes are eliminated. 
source: victoriaboler.com
Full Rhythm: tt tt t t tt tt t z tt tt t t t t t z

Title: Big Fat Biscuit
Source: Eisen, A. & Robertson, L. (2002). An American Methodology. Lake Charles, LA: Sneaky Snake Publications
Classroom Use: Introduce tam-ti, Practice low la, Practice syn-co-pa
Game:
Students form a standing horseshoe, facing the center. At the open end, a line with tape is drawn on the ground.
At the start of the song, the two students at the end of the horseshoe step up to the line. At the end on ‘'Jump!” the two students jump as far as they can. The jumpers must start with flat feet, no running start. If a student stumbles or steps out to balance after their jump, they are disqualified. The farthest jumper is the winner, and both jumpers rejoin the horseshoe in the middle. Repeat with new jumpers.

Title: Chickama Craney Crow
Source: Aubin, N. et al. (1981). Silver Burdett Music, Early Childhood. Englewood Cliffs, NJ. Silver Burdett.
Game:
Students form a line with their hands on the shoulders of the student in front of them, with the leader being “Craney Crow”. A “Witch” is selected to hide in the corner of the room.
Craney Crow leads the Chickens in a march around the front of the Witch's hiding place, singing in a taunting manner.
At the words, “What time, Old Witch?” the Witch rushes from her hideout, seizes a Chicken, takes it to her hiding place, and calls out “One.”
The Chickens again form a line behind Craney Crow and the game is resumed from the beginning. As the Witch seizes the second Chicken, she answers to the question, “What time, Old Witch?” the word “Two,” and soon “Three, Four, etc.,” until twelve Chickens have been seized. In case the number of players is small, the Witch seizes half of Craney Crow's Chickens.
After this has been done, the singing stops; the Witch draws a line on the ground with a stick, and the Chickens behind the Witch and the Chickens behind Craney Crow pull against each other. The first child pulled across the line on the Witch's side becomes the new Witch; the first child pulled across the line on Craney Crow's side becomes the new Craney Crow, and the game is begun again from the very beginning.
source: kodalycollection.org, Altona Trent-Jones, Play Songs of the Deep South, 1944.
Full Rhythm: ttt ttt t ti tum ttt t ti t ti tum ttt t ti ttt t ti t ti t ti tum
PDFs: Chickama Craney Crow-Traditional_Notation.pdf, Chickama Craney Crow-Stick_Notation.pdf, Chickama Craney Crow-Stick_Notation_no_melody.pdf
Lyrics: Chickama chickama Craney crow, Went to the well to wash his toe, When he got there his chicken was gone, what time, what time, old witch? ONE!

Title: Oboshinotentoten
Source: Learned from aileensmusicroom.com. Retrieved 2025
Game:
Students form a seated circle, with student's left hands facing up and right hand, palm-up, in their neighbor's left hand.
Students pass the beat by crossing their body with their right hand and tapping the hand of the neighbor to their left. At the end of the song, students count out “1, 2, 3, 4, 5.” while passing the beat and speeding up. The student who gets tapped on 5 is out, unless they are quick enough to dodge the hand and the student who said 4 is out.

Title: Sarasponda
Source: Beall, P. & Nipp S. (1982). Wee Sing Around the Campfire. Los Angeles, CA. Price Stern Sloan
Game:
Students pair up and sit across from each other, each holding a rhythm stick in each hand. They are assigned A and B
Bt. 1 - Students tap the sticks on the ground beside them
Bt. 2 - Students clap their own sticks
Bt. 3 - Students click their R stick with each other
Bt. 4 - Students clap their own sticks
Bt. 5 - Students click their L stick with each other
Bts. 6-7 - A holds their sticks up shoulder length apart, B readies their sticks in between. They click their sticks 3 times to ‘ret-set-set', A moving in-out-in and B moving out-in-out, moving their sticks past each other with each click.
Bt. 8 - Rest, students raise their sticks high to prepare to tap the ground
Bts. 9-16 - Repeat beats 1-8
Bt. 17 - Students toss R stick in the air making one full rotation on ‘do'.
Bt. 19.5 - Students clap own sticks together as two 8th notes in the space of the rest
Bt. 20 - Students toss L stick in the air making one full rotation on ‘do'.
Bt. 22.5 - Students clap own sticks together as two 8th notes in the space of the rest
Bt. 25 - Students tap the sticks on the ground beside them
Bt. 26 - Students clap their own sticks
Bt. 27-28 - Repeat beats 6-7, in-out-in/out-in-out movement
Bt. 29 - Students tap the sticks on the ground beside them
Bt. 30 - Students clap their own sticks
Bt. 31 - Students clap their partner sticks
Increase the difficulty: For beats 17 and 20 instead of tossing and catching their own stick, the students can toss them to each other and catch them
Full Rhythm: tt tt tt tt tt tt tt t tt tt tt tt tt tt tt t t tmt t tiz ti tt tt t tiz ti tt tt tt t tt tt t z
PDFs: Sarasponda-Traditional_Notation.pdf, Sarasponda-Stick_Notation.pdf, Sarasponda-Stick_Notation_no_melody.pdf
Lyrics: Sarasponda sarasponda sarasponda ret set set. Sarasponda sarasponda sarasponda ret set set. Adorayoh, Adorayboomdayoh. Adorayboomday ret set set, Ah say pah say oh.

Title: Shanghai Chicken
Source: Scarborough, D. (1925). On the Trail of Negro Folk-Songs. Cambridge, MA. Harvard University Press
Classroom Use: Introduce tika-tika, Practice syn-co-pa, Practice low so
Game:
Students form a seated circle, each with an egg shaker.
Beat 1: egg taps the right knee
Bt. 2: egg taps left knee
Bt. 3: egg taps the right knee
Bt. 4: egg taps left knee
Bt. 5: toss egg
Bt. 6: catch egg
Bt. 7: toss egg
Bt. 8: catch egg
Bts. 9-14: same actions as beats 1-6
Bt. 15: toss egg to neighbor to the right
Bt. 16: catch egg
Repeat.  This can become an elimination game, where players that drop eggs are "out."  "Outed" players can then play an ostinato on a non-pitched percussion instrument or form a new circle.
source: Leigh Ann Mock Garner (learned from Susan Brumfield), musicalaabbott.com
Full Rhythm: tt tktk tt t scp to tt tt tt t scp to

Title: Ye Toop Doram
Source: Composed by American Marilyn Shepard, inspired by an Afghan Folksong found in Walker, M. (1983). World of Children's Songs. Washington DC. Friendship Press
Classroom Use: Practice tam-ti, Practice syn-co-pa, Practice low fa, Practice natural minor, Language: Farsi (Persian), Afghan culture
Game:
Students form a seated circle. One student starts with a ball and one student is selected to stand in the middle.
Students pass the ball twice per measure (to the half note). Freeze the ball on “yek, doh, se,”. Students whack the ball, pinball flipper style, to try to roll the ball to hit the middle student's feet. The middle student must try to dodge the ball for as long as possible. The game ends when the student's feet are hit, or for X number of repeats.
Increase the difficulty: Add more balls during the rolling phase.

Title: Four White Horses
Source: Locke, E. (2004). Sail Away: 155 American Folk Songs to Sing, Read and Play. New York, NY. Boosey & Hawkes.
Game:
Students pair up. Two sets of partners form a square, partners stand across from each other, pair A and pair B.
Each number = 8 claps on the beat
M. 1 – Students “A” do high five, Students “B” do “low five”
M. 3 – All clap own hands
M. 5 – “A” – low five, “B” – high five
M. 7 – All clap own hands
M. 9 – Turn to corner person, high five
M. 11 – Clap own hands
M. 13 – All clap “side five”
Increase the difficulty: Decrease the number of beats of each action from 8 to 4 to 2 to 1.

Title: Zudio
Source: Collected from Barbara Woodring. Chicago, IL.
Classroom Use: Practice syn-co-pa, Practice low la, Swing 8ths
Game:
Students form 2 lines of partners facing each other.
“Here we go, Zudio”: Partners join hands and move arms in a sawing motion. 
“Step back, Sally”: Partners drop hands and jump backwards on the beat, clapping on the offbeat. 
“Goin' down the alley”: Head couple dances down the “alley” and rejoins lines at the foot; meanwhile, others step sideways twice toward the top of the set, continuing to clap on the offbeat.

Title: Cut the Cake
Source: Choksy, L. (1988). 120 Singing Games and Dances for Elementary Schools. Upper Saddle River, NJ. Prentice Hall
Game:
Students form a standing circle. The teacher chooses a cake cutter (CC) to stand outside of the circle.
M. 1 – Students in the circle clap on the steady beat. The CC walks around outside of the circle.
M. 3 – Students shake (in place)
M. 5 – Students hold hands
End – The CC stops on the word “cake”. The CC does a chop on the grasped hands between two students. Those two students run away from the CC in opposite directions around the circle to try to reach the spot where they were standing. The CC holds up their hands for a high high-five. The first runner to high-five becomes the new CC. Note: when the runners are going around the circle, they must pass each other. Encourage them to high-five each other to avoid a cakey collision. 
source: bethsnotesplus.com
Full Rhythm: tt tt t t tt tt t z tt tt t tt tt tt t z
PDFs: Cut the Cake-Traditional_Notation.pdf, Cut the Cake-Stick_Notation.pdf, Cut the Cake-Stick_Notation_no_melody.pdf

Title: Great Big House
Source: Locke, E. (2004). Sail Away: 155 American Folk Songs to Sing, Read and Play. New York, NY. Boosey & Hawkes.
Game:
Students form a standing circle and are given an alternating number 1 or 2.
Verse 1
M. 1 – Ones step forward 4 steps (on the quarter note beats)
M. 3 – Ones step back 4 steps
M. 5 – Twos step forward 4 steps
M. 7 – Twos step back 4 steps
Verse 2
M. 1 – Ones step forward 4 steps and join hands
M. 3 – Twos step forward 4 steps while reaching into the circle created by the Ones. They join hands with other Twos.
M. 5 – Twos raise their joined hands over the heads of the Ones and stand behind the Ones' circle.
M. 7 – Ones raise their joined hands over the heads of the Twos and stand intertwined
Verse 3
M. 1 – All students – left foot step left, bring right foot to step together with left foot. (4 times)
M. 5 – Ones lift their hands over the heads of the Twos
M. 7 – Twos circle left 4 small steps until they are each in between 2 different people
source: bethsnotesplus.com
Full Rhythm: tt tt tt t tt tt t t tt tt tt t tt tt t z
PDFs: Great Big House-Traditional_Notation.pdf, Great Big House-Stick_Notation.pdf, Great Big House-Stick_Notation_no_melody.pdf
Lyrics: Great big house in New Orleans, Forty stories high, Ev'ry room that I've been in, filled with pumpkin pie. Went down to the old mill stream, To fetch a pail of water Put one arm around my wife, The other 'round my daughter. Title: Here Comes a Bluebird
Source: Erdei, P. & Komlos, K. (1974). 150 American Folk Songs to Sing, Read, and Play. New York, NY. Boosey and Hawkes.
Game:
Students form a standing circle, holding raised hands. The teacher selects the “Bluebird” who stands outside the circle.
As students sing, Bluebird flies in and out of the circle (under the raised hands of the students). On “take a little partner,” Bluebird chooses a partner, and both students go in the middle of the circle and hop in place. The chosen partner becomes the new Bluebird.
Alternate ending: Both students remain as Bluebirds, and the amount of birds grows exponentially. 
source: bethsnotesplus.com, C. Roberts, Kodaly Level 1 materials

Title: Weevily Wheat
Source: Sandburg, C. (1927). The American Songbag. New York, NY. Harcourt Brace and Company
Classroom Use: Practice syn-co-pa, Practice tika-ti, Practice low so, Classroom: Math
Game:
Students form standing circle groups of 4. Before starting, each student gets a number 1,2,3 or 4.
M. 1 – All circle left
M. 3 – All circle right
M. 5 – Face center. On each beat, each student adds right hand to the middle, stacking hands on top of each other. (1,2,3,4)
M. 6 – On each beat, each student adds their left hand to the stack. (1,2,3,4)
M. 7 – On each beat, each student stacks their right hand on top, pulling their hand from the bottom of the pile. (1,2,3,4)
M. 8 – On each beat, each student stacks their left hand on top, pulling their hand from the bottom of the pile. (1,2,3,4)

Title: Over the River Charlie
Source: Library of Congress AFS 01732 A4 Collected by Alan and Elizabeth Lomax. Variation from Beth's Notes.
Classroom Use: Practice ti-tika, Practice tika-ti, Practice low te, Learn mixolydian
Game:
Students form two long lines facing each other. The two students at the end of each line are the head couple.
Refrain:
Ms. 1-4 = Head couple sashay down between the two lines.
Ms. 5-8 = Head couple sashay up to where they started.
Verse 1:
Ms. 9-12 = Head couple right arm swing 1.5 times around
Ms. 13-16 = Head couple left arm swing one student from the opposite line.
Refrain: Repeat Ms. 9-16 with next students in the lines.
The head couple continues in the same way until all students have had a turn to swing the head couple. Then, the Head couple stands at the end of each line and a new head couple begins the sequence.
source: bethsnotesplus.com
Full Rhythm: ttk ttk tt t ttk tt tt t ttk ttk tt t tt tt t t tt tt tt t tt tt tt t tt tt ttk tmk tt tt t t

Title: Charlie Over the Ocean
Source: Courlander, H. (1951). Negro Folk Music of Alabama. Folkways album FE 4474. This is a variation with simplified rhythms.
Type: Call and Response, Circle Game, Chase Game, Solo Singing
Game:
Students form a standing circle, a “Charlie” chosen to stand outside the circle.
Charlie walks around the circle during the song while solo singing the Call. They may make up a new ocean animal to replace ‘big fish'. At the end of the song, Charlie stops walking and the student closest to them stands and chases them around the circle. Charlie tries to escape and sit in the empty spot. The chaser becomes the new Charlie.
Increase the difficulty: Closer to the game in the original source, the students are form a standing circle and skip to the right during the song. Charlie is outside the circle and skips to the left. The chasing element remains the same.
source: Courlander, H. Ring Games, Line Games and Play Party Songs of Alabama.

Game:
Students form a standing circle, holding hands. A “Cat” stands inside the circle and an “Owner”, pretending to be and old man or woman, stands outside the circle.
Students sing while walking to the beat in a circle. The Cat meows on the rests. At the end of the song, the students lift their hands and the Owner chases the Cat in and out of the arches until the Cat is caught. Repeat with a new Cat and Owner.

Title: Serra, Serra, Serrador
Source: Campbell, P.S., McCollough-Barbson, E. & Tucker, J.C. (1994). Roots and Branches. Danbury, CT. World Music Press
Classroom Use: Practice so/mi, Practice ti-tika, Language: Portuguese
Game:
Possible game: Students form a seated circle, with student's left hands facing up and right hand, palm-up, in their neighbor's left hand.
Students pass the beat by crossing their body with their right hand and tapping the hand of the neighbor to their left. At the end of the song, students count out to 10 in Portuguese while passing the beat and speeding up. The student who gets tapped on ‘dez' is out, unless they are quick enough to dodge the hand and the student who said ‘nove' is out.

Title: Diddle Diddle Dumpling
Source: Opie I. & Opie P. (1951). The Oxford Dictionary of Nursery Rhymes (1st ed.). Oxford, UK. Oxford University Press.
Game:
Possible game, inspired from Zapatitos Blancos: Students form a seated circle with both legs extended in front of them. The teacher or a student leader is chosen to be inside the circle.
The leader pats a steady beat around the circle, tapping each student's shoe as everyone speaks the rhyme. On the last beat of the rhyme, that person's foot is eliminated from the game. They tuck it underneath them, out of the circle, so one foot is still in the game and one foot is out. Continue until all shoes are eliminated.

Title: Sasara ang Bulaklak
Source: Factora, M. (2024). Musical Folk Games of Manila, Philippines. Manila, PH. Aweng Publication.
Classroom Use: Practice low so, Practice low la, Language: Tagalog
Game:
Students form a standing circle, holding hands. A student, “It”, is chosen to stand in the middle of the circle.
M. 1 - The circle steps towards It in the middle
M. 3 - The circle steps back out to full arms width
Ms. 5-8 - Repeat first 4 measures
M. 9 - It closes their eyes and spins in a circle, pointing outwards
End - At the end of the song, It stops spinning, and that students becomes the new It.

Title: We Are Dancing in the Forest
Source: Locke, Eleanor G., American Folk Songs for Teaching. Unpublished collection, Holy Names University. Collected by Betsy Moll.
Game:
Students stand on one side of the room, a “Wolf” is chosen to stand in the middle.
After singing the song, the students ask “Wolf, are you there?”. The wolf can say “No,” and say what he is doing instead (running, swimming, dancing, ect.) while acting out the motion. The wolf can say “Yes!” and the students must race to the other side of the room without getting caught by the Wolf. If a student is caught, they are the new Wolf.
Variation: Both students can stay Wolves and grow their pack exponentially.

Title: Pala Palita
Source: Knowles, F. (1990s). Vamos a Cantar: 230 Latino and Hispanic Folksongs to Sing, Read, and Play. Columbus, OH. Kodaly Institute at Capital University.
Classroom Use: Practice so/mi, Practice ta ti-ti, Language: Spanish
Game:
Students partner up, or form small groups.
This game is played by two or more who balance a narrow, flat stick about six or seven inches long crosswise over a long stick, which is on the ground. On one end of the flat stick any number of small objects are placed, an equal number for each player. The pieces are marked on each side to represent heads and tails (caras y cruces). To start the game, each player tosses a piece at a mark on the ground several feet away, and the one coming closest with his/her piece playes first. Calling "cara' (heads) or "cruz" (tails), he/she strikes with his/her hand the end of the stick opposite the pieces, flipping them backward. If he/she chose "heads" he/she keeps all those that are "heads up" and places the rest of them on the end of the stick. The other players then take their turns until all the pieces are won.
source: C. Roberts, Kodaly Level 1 materials
Full Rhythm: t tt t tt tt tt t t
PDFs: Pala Palita-Traditional_Notation.pdf, Pala Palita-Stick_Notation.pdf, Pala Palita-Stick_Notation_no_melody.pdf

Title: Tarzan the Monkey Man
Source: Dartmouth Folklore Archive, accessed 2/26. Video informant Colwell C on 11/16.
Game:
Students form a seated circle, with student's left hands facing up and right hand, palm-up, in their neighbor's left hand.
Students pass the beat by crossing their body with their right hand and tapping the hand of the neighbor to their left. At the end of the song, the student just tapped chooses a color. Students spell to the chosen color a letter at a time while continuing the pass hand pattern. The final student tapped is out, and can create a new circle inside the larger circle.

Title: Sagidi, Sapopo
Source: Factora, M. (2024). Musical Folk Games of Manila, Philippines. Manila, PH. Aweng Publication.
Game:
Students form a standing circle. One student stands in the center.
In the first measure, the student in the middle creates a movement to the rhythm. The students in the circle copy them in the following measures.
Increase the difficulty: The students copy the movement while the student in the middle creates a new movement in measure 2. This continues in canon, the circle students always copying 4 beats after the center student.

Title: Down Came a Lady
Source: Trinka, J. (1989). Folksongs, Singing Games, and Play Parties. Volume III. Chicago, IL. GIA Publications Inc.
Game:
Students form a standing circle, holding hands. One student stands in the center.
While singing, students circle right. At the end of the song, the center student points to someone in the circle and all replace “blue” with the color they are wearing (optionally solo sung). The selected student leaves the circle and walks on the outside in the opposite direction. Repeat until all students have formed a new circle on the outside. During the final repetition, all students in the circle point to the student in the middle and sing the name of the color they are wearing.
source: Trinka, J. (1989). Folksongs, Singing Games, and Play Parties. Volume III.

Alternate game: Students are in a standing or seated circle. They sing the song and the teacher sings the color at the end, and students must point and guess who the teacher is referring to.
Full Rhythm: t tt t t t t t z t tt tt tt tt tt t z

Title: I've Been to Harlem
Source: Trinka, J. (1989). Folksongs, Singing Games, and Play Parties. Chicago, IL. GIA Publications Inc. Modified for clarity.
Game:
Played with an odd number of students. Students form two concentric standing circles. Partners hold each others' hands in skating position. The single student stands in the middle.
M. 1-7: Partners walk around counterclockwise on the half note beat. The single student walks the opposite way or wanders around.
M. 8: On ‘over', partners wring the dishrag.
M. 9-15: Partners drop hands. Outside circle continues walking counterclockwise, inner circle turns and walks clockwise. The single students joins the inner circle.
M. 16: On the final ‘cean' of ‘ocean', the inner circle must quickly find an outer circle partner. One player will be left with no partner, and the game repeats.
source: Trinka, J. (1989). Folksongs, Singing Games, and Play Parties.

Title: Bingo
Source: Trinka, J. (1989). Folksongs, Singing Games, and Play Parties. Chicago, IL. GIA Publications Inc.
Game:
Students form a standing circle, holding hands. Optionally decide adjacent partner pairs if practicing a grand right left.
M. 1-4: circle left
M. 5-8: circle right
M. 9-10: 4 steps in
M. 11-12: 4 steps out
M. 13-16: repeat measures 9-12
After the song is over, spell BINGO and perform movements of your choice or use to practice a grand right left by passing your partner with every letter spelled.

Title: Cedar Swamp
Source: Trinka, J. (1989). Folksongs, Singing Games, and Play Parties. Chicago, IL. GIA Publications Inc.
Game:
Students form two lines of partners facing each other.
During the verse, the top couple sashays 8 beats down the set. At the end of the set, they right arm swing for 4 beats, then left arm swing for 4 beats.
During the chorus, all partners right arm swing 4 beats, left arm swing 4 beats, repeat. There is now a new top couple.

Title: Mazoo, Mazoo
Source: Trinka, J. (2008). Folksongs, Singing Games, and Play Parties. Chicago, IL. GIA Publications Inc.
Classroom Use: Practice syn-co-pa, Introduce tam-ti, Practice fa
Game:
Students form a standing circle, one student stands on the inside.
Verse 1: The center student walks around the inside of the circle facing the other students while doing washing motions with their hand. The circle students can match their washing motion.
Verse 2: The center student continues walking and washing, and stops to face a partner at the end of the verse.
Verse 3: The two partners “hustle” by performing a jig step, hopping from one foot to the other while optionally pumping their arms up and down. The group can accelerate in tempo.
Both the original student and the new partner are now inside the circle, and Verses 1-3 are repeated until every student is selected.
source: Trinka, J. (2008). Folksongs, Singing Games, and Play Parties.
Full Rhythm: ti tt tt scp tmt t tiz ti tt tt scp tt tt t

Title: The Noble Duke of York
Source: Trinka, J. (1989). Folksongs, Singing Games, and Play Parties. Chicago, IL. GIA Publications Inc.
Game:
Students form 2 standing lines facing each other.
Verse 1: Top couple sashays down the center to the bottom of the set, and back to the top.
Verse 2: Top couple creates a movement to commute to the bottom of the set (walk, dance, ect.) and creates an arch with both sets of hands joined high above their heads by the end of the verse.
Verse 3: The next top couple joins hands as they walk down the center towards the arch. Each couple follows them through the arch and peels off back towards their original side. The arch couple stays at the bottom of the set and a new top couple prepares for repetition of verses 1-3.
source: Trinka, J. (1989). Folksongs, Singing Games, and Play Parties.
Full Rhythm: tmk tt tt t tiz ti tt tt t tiz ti tt ttk ttk tt tt tt t

Title: A Hunting We Will Go
Source: Variation of Verse 3 of “The Noble Duke of York”: Trinka, J. (1989). Folksongs, Singing Games, and Play Parties. Chicago, IL. GIA Publications Inc.
Game:
Students form a standing circle. Two students are chosen to be the first gate, hands joined together and held up high for students to walk through.
As the students sing, walk in a circle with students going under the gate. On the word “catch”, the gate comes down and any students caught in the middle are added to the gate which becomes bigger and bigger as the song repeats with new lyrics.
Variation: Instead of adding on to one gate, any captured students form a new gate of two students each. If there is an odd number, the solo student can wait for a partner to complete their gate.

Title: P. P. Pollyanna
Source: Trinka, J. (1989). Folksongs, Singing Games, and Play Parties. Chicago, IL. GIA Publications Inc.
Game:
Students form a standing circle, one student stands on the inside.
M. 1-8: Center student skips around the inside of the circle
M 9-10: Center student stops in front of one student. Both put their hands on their hips.
M. 11-12: Both partners fold arms, clap hands once
M. 13-14: Both partners imitate a Scottish dance: One hand on hip, other hand raised, one foot raised flamingo style, hop on the other foot
M. 15-16: Both parters quickly bow and juts out their bottom, imagining a kilt flying upwards
Repeat the song now with 2 students in the center (the original center student and their partner). Repeat until everyone is in the center.

Title: Riding in a Buggy
Source: Trinka, J. (1989). Folksongs, Singing Games, and Play Parties. Chicago, IL. GIA Publications Inc.
Game:
Students form two concentric circles, facing a partner. Each set of partners joins hands in promenade position.
Verses: Partners walk to the beat counterclockwise.
Chorus: Students stop walking and face their partner. They perform the 4 beat pattern: pat own thighs, clap own hands, clap partner's hands, clap own hands.
Before the next verse begins, the inner circle takes one step to the left to face a new partner and ready their hands in the promenade position.
source: Trinka, J. (1989). Folksongs, Singing Games, and Play Parties.

Title: Skating Away
Source: Trinka, J. (1989). Folksongs, Singing Games, and Play Parties. Chicago, IL. GIA Publications Inc.
Game:
Students form a standing circle. Four students are chosen to stand inside the circle.
Verse 1: The students in the center put their right hand in to form a RH star. The center students skip to the right. The outer circle skip left, the opposite direction to the center.
Verse 2: The center students quickly switch arms and skip in a LH star. The The outer circle skip right, the opposite direction to the center. 
Verse 3: Each one of the center students picks a new student in the circle. They hook right arms and perform a RH swing. The old partner retires to the circle and the 4 new partners meet in the middle. The other students in the circle and clapping a steady beat.
Repeat with the 4 new center students.
Variation: The old partners do not rejoin the circle, instead both old and new groups of 4 are in the circle and the number of center students grows exponentially. 
source: Trinka, J. (1989). Folksongs, Singing Games, and Play Parties.
Full Rhythm: ti t ti ttt ttt t ti ttt t ti ttt t ti t ti ttt ttt t ti t ti ttt tum t

Title: Wishy Washy
Source: Trinka, J. (1989). Folksongs, Singing Games, and Play Parties. Chicago, IL. GIA Publications Inc.
Game:
Students form a standing circle. Two students are chosen to stand inside the circle, they are sailing the ship.
The outside circle sings and claps a steady beat.
The center partners face each other and lock hands. Their four feet are close together in the middle, and they lean backwards. The students spin in a circle around their feet for the first 2 phrases.
For verses 3-4, each student walks up to someone in the circle and puts their hand on their shoulders, and the chosen partner follows suit. Both students hop a side-to-side kick-step (hop on RF and LF goes out to the side, hop on LF and RF goes out to the side).
The two new chosen students enter the center and prepare to spin as the song repeats.
source: Trinka, J. (1989). Folksongs, Singing Games, and Play Parties.

Title: Dry Bones
Source: Variation on Library of Congress AFS 105 B2. Collected by John A. Lomax.
Type: Spiritual, Passing Game, Call and Response, Solo Singing
Game:
Students form a seated circle. Start a passing pattern with each students' right hand tapping in front of them, then in front of the student to their right.
For the first 2 phrases, pass an object to the right to the quarter note beat. At the end of the second phrase, the student holds onto the object that landed in front of them.
For the last 2 phrases, the chosen student taps the object on their knee to a steady beat. Their name is replaced in the song “Some of them bones are __'s bones,” which the group of students sing as a call. The chosen student responds with “Some of them bones are mine”.
Repeat the song and play continues.
source: Patti Anderson blog sharing a workshop from Gary Arnold

Title: Goin' To Boston
Source: Trinka, J. (2008). My Little Rooster. Chicago, IL. GIA Publications Inc.
Game:
Students form a double line of partners in promenade position, “girls” on the right.
Verse 1: All promenade to the right, then return to the beginning position with the head couple at the top.
Verse 2: All promenade to the left, returning as above.
Verse 3: The head couple joins hands, leads down between the lines, then returns to the top.
Verse 4: The head couple joins right hands and begins a grand right and left around the circle in opposite directions. All the men follow the leading man, continuing the grand right and left around the circle. The women do the same. The head couple returns to place.
The chorus is sung for the first time after this figure, as the other couples return to place.
Verse 5: Virginia Reel Starting from the original formation, the head couple swings each other by the right arm. They then move to the opposite line and swing the next person in line, then return to the middle to swing each other again. The head couple then moves to the opposite line again to swing the next person farther down the line. Continue this pattern until reaching the end of the line.
The chorus and Verse 6 may also be sung if additional music is needed for the head couple to complete the reel.
Verse 6 (for extra music): The head couple remains at the bottom of the set, and the dance begins again with a new head couple.
source: kodalycollection.org

Title: I Don't Care if the Rain Comes Down
Source: Z. Patty. (1974). Bullfrog Ballades. Downtown DLJ Songs.
Game:
Students form a standing circle. Number everyone one, two, one, two all the way around. Identify your first partner:
Ones: First partner is on the right.
Twos: First partner is on the left.
Line 1 and 2:
Tap knees.
Clap hands.
Clap hands with your first partner.
Clap.
Tap knees.
Clap hands.
Clap hands with your second partner.
Clap.
Line 3:
Ones: Step forward on the right foot, bring the left foot to meet it. Step to the right in front of your partner, bring the left foot to meet it. Then take one step forward and bring the right foot to meet the left.
Twos: Take one step to the left, bring the right foot to meet it. Then take one step forward and bring the right foot to meet the left.
Line 4:
Ones: Step backward on the right foot, bring the left foot to meet it. Step to the right behind your partner, bring the left foot to meet it.
Twos: Take one step to the left, bring the right foot to meet it. Then take one step backward and bring the right foot to meet the left.
At the end of the sequence, everyone should have changed places.

source: Z. Patty. (1974). Bullfrog Ballades.
Full Rhythm: tt ttk tt t ttk tt to tt ttk tt t ttk tt to t t tktk t ttk tt to t t tktk t ttk tt to

Lyrics: I don't care if the rain comes down. I'm gonna dance all day. I don't care if the rain comes down. I'm gonna dance all day. Hey, Ho! Carry me away, I'm gonna dance all day. Hey, Ho! Carry me away, I'm gonna dance all day.

Title: I'se the B'y
Source: Fowke, E. (1973). Penguin Book of Canadian Folk Songs. Markham, Ontario: Penguin Books
Game:
Students form a standing circle of boy-girl (or “one-two”) partners 
mm. 1-4: Girls take four steps in and four steps out; mm. 5-8: Boys take four steps in and four steps out; mm. 9-10: Lightly bump hips with partner; mm. 11-12: Lightly bump hips with corner; mm. 13-16: Right-arm swing with your partner, switching places at the end. 

source: Trinka, J.

Hip yer partner, Sally Tibbo, Hip yer partner Sally Brown; Fogo, Twillingate, Morton's harbour, All around the circle. Sods and rinds to cover your flakes, Cakes and tea for supper,Codfish in the spring of the year, Fried in maggoty butter. Title: Tamborês
Source: Learned from Julianna Barker Gylseth, UFOLKS.
Game:
Students pair up. Each set of partners standing facing each other.
Clapping game to the rhythm of ta, ta, ti-ti, ta 
Beat 1: Grab R fist with L hand
Beat 2: Grab R elbow with L hand
Beats 3 &: Clap own hands, clap partners hands 
Beat 4: pat legs with both hands. 
Repeat this pattern, switching to the opposite hands for each movement. Alternate the two 4 beat patterns. 
Alternative: Start students by teaching and performing the pattern in half time to the song
source: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Fn09odR1RFQ

Title: Who Stole My Chickens?
Source: Waterhouse, C; Allen, L.; Compton, E.; Hollins, N. How Can I Keep From Singing! British Kodály Society, 2007.  Clapping game attributed to Bruce and Julie Swank.
Game:
Students pair up. Optionally this can be played in concentric circles, with the outer circle moving to the right after each repeat.
1st time: pat own knees (or pat lap), clap own hands, clap right hands, clap own hands, -clap left hands, clap own hands, -clap both hands with partners, clap own hands
2nd time: extend left elbow and cup partners left help with right hand (left hand should be placed on own left shoulder) -clap own hands -reverse first step -clap own hands
3rd time: place right hand flat out, palm up. Left hand pretends to grab food from their partner's palm and eat it. Repeat, reversing hands each time.
source: Bruce and Julie Swank. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-zN3F4Tp3Y0
